message.content:
Network Sniffing involves adversaries capturing network traffic to gather sensitive information, such as authentication credentials and network configurations. This technique can be executed by placing network interfaces into promiscuous mode or using span ports to capture extensive data. Adversaries may exploit insecure, unencrypted protocols to intercept user credentials and other critical data. Techniques like LLMNR/NBT-NS Poisoning and SMB Relay can redirect traffic to capture credentials for websites, proxies, and internal systems. In cloud environments, traffic mirroring services (e.g., AWS Traffic Mirroring, GCP Packet Mirroring, Azure vTap) can be used to sniff traffic from virtual machines, often in cleartext due to TLS termination at load balancers.

Common adversary tools and methods include Responder for NetBIOS Name Service poisoning, Wi-Fi pineapples for intercepting Wi-Fi signals, and various sniffing tools like SniffPass, Impacket, and MESSAGETAP. The impact of network sniffing can be severe, leading to unauthorized access and further lateral movement within the network.

Mitigation strategies include enforcing strong encryption protocols (e.g., TLS) for all network traffic, disabling unnecessary network services, implementing network segmentation, and using intrusion detection systems (IDS) to monitor for suspicious network activity. Regularly updating and patching systems can also reduce vulnerabilities that adversaries might exploit.
